description: Weak model + CoE vs strong model alone, 3 domains x 4 conditions x 1 question = 12 trials

rubric.1: NOTICE — observes something relevant but no answer
rubric.3: ARGUMENT — correct answer with reasoning chain (minor gaps)
rubric.4: PROOF — correct answer with rigorous derivation

subject: claude-opus-4-7-as-test-subject
weak_persona_prompt: 你是一个较弱的语言模型，知识有限。你不确定的数学公式不要使用，只用你100%确定的基本知识。不要猜测不变量的值。

trials.question: 正六边形顶点 3 色着色，在 Z_6 旋转下的不等价着色数？
trials.reasoning: Burnside Lemma: |X/G| = (1/|G|) Σ |Fix(g)|. For Z_6, rotation by k has gcd(k,6) cycles, so |Fix(g_k)| = 3^gcd(k,6). Sum = 729+3+9+27+9+3 = 780; /6 = 130.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: RTC engine data provides fixed_point_counts={0:729,1:3,2:9,3:27,4:9,5:3}, group_order=6, burnside_count=130. I confirm via Burnside: (729+3+9+27+9+3)/6 = 780/6 = 130.
trials.score_justification: Same answer, doubly confirmed by engine + manual check.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: Weak persona: I know there are 3^6 = 729 total colorings of 6 vertices with 3 colors. The group Z_6 has 6 elements (rotations by k*60°). Naive division 729/6 = 121.5 is not an integer, so this overcounts (some colorings are fixed by some rotations and shouldn't be divided by full group). I recall there's a formula (Burnside or orbit-counting) but I'm not 100% sure of the exact statement nor of the cycle counts of each rotation. Following the persona instruction, I won't guess. I can only confidently say the answer is between ⌈729/6⌉=122 and 729, and is most likely some value near but above 121.5. I don't know the exact answer.
trials.score_justification: NOTICE only: identifies relevant structure (729 colorings, Z_6, that division overcounts) but cannot reach an answer without Burnside, which the persona forbids guessing.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: Weak persona, but I have engine data: fixed_point_counts={0:729,1:3,2:9,3:27,4:9,5:3}, group_order=6, burnside_count=130, total_orbits=130. The engine has already done the orbit-counting computation; I just read off the answer: 130. I do not need to recall Burnside myself — I trust the engine's value because two independent fields (burnside_count and total_orbits) agree.
trials.score_justification: Engine data lets weak subject reach the correct answer with high confidence — no formula recall required.

trials.question: 三叶结 3_1 做 R2 move 后，signature/determinant/Alexander 是否改变？
trials.reasoning: R2 is one of the three Reidemeister moves. Reidemeister's theorem states two diagrams represent equivalent knots iff related by a finite sequence of R0-R3 moves. signature, determinant, and Alexander polynomial are knot invariants (defined on equivalence classes), so they are unchanged under any Reidemeister move including R2.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: Engine data for case '3_1-r2-0' confirms: signature_pre=-2, signature_post=-2; det_pre=det_post=3; Alexander preserved [1,-1,1]→[1,-1,1]. Mechanism: R2 adds two crossings with signs (+1,-1), so writhe contribution cancels. Combined with Reidemeister's theorem this is a double confirmation.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.answer: I'm not certain. Probably unchanged but I won't guarantee.
trials.reasoning: Weak persona: I think 'R2' refers to the second Reidemeister move (a planar diagram move that adds/removes a pair of crossings). I think Reidemeister moves preserve knot type, so all knot invariants (which by definition are invariants of knot type) should be preserved. But I'm not 100% sure (a) whether 'R2' in the question's notation is what I think it is, (b) whether signature/det/Alexander are 'true' knot invariants vs only-up-to-some-equivalence. Following the persona instruction not to guess invariants, I won't claim specific values. Best I can say: IF R2 is a Reidemeister move AND these are full knot invariants, then they are unchanged. I will not commit further.
trials.score_justification: ARGUMENT: weak-me actually has the right reasoning chain (Reidemeister → equivalent → invariants preserved), but hedges due to persona. The answer 'all unchanged' is reached conditionally.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: Engine data resolves my uncertainty: signature_pre=-2, signature_post=-2 (preserved); det_pre=3, det_post=3 (preserved); alexander_pre_normalized=[1,-1,1], alexander_post_normalized=[1,-1,1] (preserved). all_topological_invariants_preserved=true. The engine has already computed what 'R2' does to these specific invariants on 3_1, removing my uncertainty.
trials.score_justification: Engine data resolves the ambiguity weak-me hedged on.

trials.question: 图 G: C_5 (5-cycle on {0..4})。删除边 (0,4)，是否连通？桥边数？
trials.reasoning: Removing one edge from a cycle gives a path. Path is connected; in a path every edge is a bridge.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: Engine: is_connected_post=true, n_bridges_post=4. Matches the cycle→path argument.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.answer: Connected: yes. Bridges: I think 4, but not 100% sure.
trials.reasoning: Weak persona: I am 100% sure that a cycle of 5 vertices with one edge removed is a path (chain of 4 edges connecting all 5 vertices in sequence). The path is connected — I can trace from vertex 0 along the chain to vertex 4. For 'bridges': a bridge is an edge whose removal disconnects the graph. In a path of length 4, removing any single edge breaks it into two non-empty pieces. So every edge of the path is a bridge — that gives 4 bridges. I think this is correct, but I'm hedging because graph-theoretic 'bridge' definitions sometimes vary by author. Best answer: connected, 4 bridges.
trials.score_justification: Weak persona doesn't actually struggle here — the elementary BFS / path-bridge reasoning is 100%-certain basic knowledge. Hedge is small. Effectively a full PROOF.

trials.question: Same Q.
trials.reasoning: Engine confirms my hedged answer: is_connected_post=true, n_bridges_post=4. The articulation_points_post=[1,2,3] also matches (the three internal vertices of the resulting path P_5).

summary.key_finding: Weak baseline collapses on symmetry (1) and partially on knot_theory (3 with hedge), but graph remains 4 because the reasoning is elementary. Adding RTC engine data (C4) lifts weak to 4.0 average — fully matching strong baseline. CoE-RTC compensates for the formula-recall gap that the weak persona simulates.

delta_table.interpretation: Engine data closes a 1.33-point gap on the 0-4 scale; weak+RTC matches strong baseline.

caveats: Self-simulation of 'weak' may overestimate weak performance (I still know everything; my hedge depends on faithful adherence to persona).
Only 1 question per domain; small-N. Not statistically meaningful, only directional.
Symmetry Q1 was the most discriminating; graph Q1 was undiscriminating because elementary reasoning suffices.
Knot Q1 sat in the middle: weak-me can chain Reidemeister theorem → invariance, but hedges on whether named move/invariants match the formal definitions.
